description: Terrain profiles for all colonizable worlds in SimSoviet 1917. Values grounded in planetary science data from NASA/ESA missions, peer-reviewed research, and IAU catalogues.

profiles.name: Siberia (Taiga/Tundra)
profiles.notes: Standard starting location. Oymyakon recorded -68C, coldest inhabited place on Earth. Growing season 90-120 days. Permafrost underlies 65% of Russian territory. Trans-Siberian Railway is the primary supply line — 9,289 km from Moscow.

profiles.name: Russian Steppe (Virgin Lands)
profiles.notes: Khrushchev's Virgin Lands Campaign (1954-1963) plowed 42 million hectares of Kazakh steppe. Initial bumper harvests followed by catastrophic soil erosion. Chernozem (black earth) is the world's most fertile soil but fragile without windbreaks. Continental extremes: -40C winters, +45C summers.

profiles.name: Luna (Near Side)
profiles.notes: 1.3-second communication delay to Earth. Lunar day/night cycle is 29.5 Earth days — 14.75 days of sunlight, 14.75 days of darkness. Shackleton crater at south pole contains permanently shadowed ice deposits. Regolith is abrasive glass-sharp dust that damages seals and lungs. Deepest crater (South Pole-Aitken) is 8.5 km deep. Luna 2 (1959) was first human object to reach the Moon — Soviet.

profiles.name: Mars (Pre-Terraforming)
profiles.notes: Olympus Mons at 21.9 km is the tallest volcano in the solar system — 2.5x Everest. Valles Marineris canyon system stretches 4,000 km, up to 7 km deep. Global dust storms can last months and reduce solar power by 99%. Subsurface water ice confirmed by SHARAD radar (Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter). Perchlorate salts in soil are toxic to humans but useful for rocket fuel. Mars 3 (1971) achieved first soft landing — Soviet.

profiles.name: Mars (Mid-Terraforming)
profiles.notes: Mid-terraforming: orbital mirrors and greenhouse gas factories have raised surface pressure to 300 mbar. Liquid water appears seasonally in equatorial channels. Atmosphere still unbreathable (95% CO2, <1% O2) but thick enough to reduce radiation significantly. Early lichens and engineered extremophile bacteria colonize sheltered valleys. Walking outdoors requires only a pressure suit and oxygen mask, not a full spacesuit.

profiles.name: Mars (Terraformed)
profiles.notes: Fully terraformed Mars with breathable atmosphere at low altitudes. Hellas Basin (7 km below datum) has thickest air — equivalent to 5,000m elevation on Earth. Olympus Mons summit still in near-vacuum. Northern ocean covers Vastitas Borealis. Low gravity means taller, thinner humans after generations. Magnetic field still absent — artificial magnetosphere at L1 Lagrange point shields atmosphere from solar wind stripping.

profiles.name: Venus (Cloud Colony, 52 km)
profiles.notes: At 52 km altitude, Venus has the most Earth-like conditions in the solar system: 1 atm pressure, 75C temperature, 0.9g gravity. Earth-normal air (N2/O2) is a lifting gas in Venus's dense CO2 atmosphere — habitats float naturally. Venus rotates retrograde (east to west) with a 243-Earth-day sidereal period, but atmospheric super-rotation gives 4-day winds at cloud level. Sulfuric acid clouds require acid-resistant habitat envelopes. Venera program (1961-1984) sent 16 Soviet probes, with Venera 7 achieving first successful landing on another planet.

profiles.name: Ganymede (Jupiter III)
profiles.notes: Largest moon in the solar system (diameter 5,268 km, larger than Mercury). Only moon with its own intrinsic magnetosphere, generated by a liquid iron core — provides partial radiation shielding. Subsurface ocean estimated at 100 km deep, sandwiched between ice layers, containing more water than all of Earth's oceans. Tidally locked to Jupiter with 7.15-day orbital period. Grooved terrain suggests past tectonic activity. JUICE mission (ESA, 2023) en route for 2031 arrival.

profiles.name: Callisto (Jupiter IV)
profiles.notes: NASA's 2003 HOPE study identified Callisto as the best location for a human base in the Jovian system due to extremely low radiation — orbiting outside Jupiter's main radiation belts, receiving only 0.01 rem/day (vs Ganymede's 0.08 and Europa's 540 rem/day). Most heavily cratered body in the solar system with a 4-billion-year-old surface — geologically dead. Valhalla impact basin is 3,800 km across. Possible subsurface ocean at 80-150 km depth. Tidally locked with 16.7-day orbital period.

profiles.name: Ceres (Asteroid Belt)
profiles.notes: Largest object in the asteroid belt (diameter 939 km), classified as a dwarf planet. Dawn spacecraft (2015-2018) revealed bright spots in Occator crater are sodium carbonate deposits from subsurface brine. Ahuna Mons is a 5-km-tall cryovolcano. Up to 25% water ice by mass — more fresh water than Earth. 9-hour rotation enables centrifugal assist for launches. Escape velocity only 0.51 km/s (vs Earth's 11.2 km/s) — ideal hub for asteroid mining operations. Strategic position between inner and outer solar system.

profiles.name: Titan (Saturn VI)
profiles.notes: Only moon with a dense atmosphere (1.47 atm, 50% thicker than Earth's). Only body besides Earth with stable surface liquid — methane/ethane lakes and seas covering the north pole. Kraken Mare is larger than the Caspian Sea. Huygens probe (ESA, 2005) landed and transmitted for 72 minutes. Thick atmosphere + low gravity = human-powered flight possible with wing-suits (1/7 Earth terminal velocity). Dragonfly rotorcraft mission launching 2028. Atmospheric methane provides virtually unlimited chemical energy. Nitrogen atmosphere means only oxygen needed for breathing — no pressure suit required, just heated insulation and O2 mask.

profiles.name: O'Neill Cylinder (Orbital Habitat)
profiles.notes: Gerard O'Neill's 1976 design: paired counter-rotating cylinders, each 8 km diameter x 32 km long. Artificial 1g via rotation at 0.53 rpm. Interior area ~1,300 km2 (half Rhode Island). Three alternating land/window strips provide natural sunlight via angled mirrors. Population capacity: 1-10 million. Closed-loop life support with 99.9% water/atmosphere recycling. Zero-g manufacturing in non-rotating hub. Coriolis effect deflects thrown objects — rain falls at an angle. Soviet Salyut program (1971-1986) pioneered long-duration space habitation.

profiles.name: Generation Ship (Interstellar Vessel)
profiles.notes: Multi-generational interstellar vessel — crew who depart are not the crew who arrive. At 10% light speed, Proxima Centauri (4.24 ly) takes 42 years; TRAPPIST-1 (39.6 ly) takes 396 years. Communication delay grows to years, then decades — eventual total isolation from Earth. Social cohesion is the primary engineering challenge: every historical analogy (Polynesian voyaging, submarine deployments, Antarctic stations) suggests community fragmentation after ~3 generations. Closed ecology must function for centuries without resupply. Tsiolkovsky (1903) first proposed multi-generation space arks — Soviet space pioneer.

profiles.name: Exoplanet (Variable Conditions)
profiles.notes: Placeholder profile with ranges for procedurally generated exoplanets. As of 2025, 5,700+ exoplanets confirmed by Kepler/TESS/JWST. TRAPPIST-1 system has 7 Earth-sized planets, 3 in habitable zone. Tidally locked worlds may have habitable terminators. M-dwarf stars (70% of all stars) produce intense flares that strip atmospheres. Native biochemistry could be incompatible (wrong chirality amino acids) or hazardous (prion-like replication). Communication with Earth is measured in years to centuries depending on distance.
